title: Length and width of Lagrangian cobordisms
speaker: Joshua Sabloff
affiliation: Haverford College; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: In this talk, I will discuss two measurements of Lagrangian cobordisms between Legendrian submanifolds in symplectizations: their length and their relative Gromov width. The Gromov width, in particular, is a fundamental global invariant of symplectic manifolds, and a relative version of that width helps understand the geometry of Lagrangian submanifolds of a symplectic manifold.

proof_stages.label: Legendrian cobordisms, length, and relative width
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes exact orientable Lagrangian cobordisms in a symplectization and defines length as the minimal non-cylindrical s-height and width as relative Gromov width after cutting off the top end.

proof_stages.label: Motivating asymmetry and main estimates
proof_stages.summary: Scaled-unknot computations motivate the two stated results: the cobordism width is bounded by twice a boundary fundamental capacity, and, when the top-end width bound is sharp, the cobordism length is bounded below by the logarithm of the ratio of end widths.

proof_stages.label: Fundamental capacity from Legendrian contact cohomology
proof_stages.summary: Using filtered linearized Legendrian contact cohomology and its duality exact sequence, a boundary fundamental class is identified and its spectral level is defined as the fundamental capacity controlling the relevant Reeb-chord action.

proof_stages.label: Seidel isomorphism for a filling
proof_stages.summary: For an exact Maslov-zero filling with its induced augmentation, wrapped Floer methods identify the homology of the filling with the linearized Legendrian contact cohomology of the boundary and send the filling point class to the boundary fundamental class.

proof_stages.label: Disk through the ball center and upper area bound
proof_stages.summary: Given a relative ball embedding, the Morse perturbation is chosen to have its maximum at the ball center so that a Seidel-isomorphism disk passes through that point, and Stokes' theorem plus the capacity filtration bounds the disk area by the fundamental capacity.

proof_stages.label: Boundary monotonicity gives the width bound
proof_stages.summary: A monotonicity argument for minimal surfaces with boundary, together with a reverse isoperimetric inequality controlling the boundary term, converts the disk-area estimate into the upper bound on relative width.

examples.name: Two cobordisms between scaled Legendrian unknots
examples.note: The speaker compares cobordisms between a Legendrian unknot with Reeb chord length 1 and a scaled one with length 2, illustrating that expanding and shrinking have different length and width behavior without being used in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws the two directed cobordisms between small and large Legendrian unknots and computes/discusses their lengths and widths, including the log 2 lower bound and why expansion versus contraction differs.

passes.reason: The objects are topologically trivial cylinder cobordisms between standard unknots, with one direction realizing a zero-length/tight boundary case used to illustrate asymmetry rather than to refute a formal claim.
the scaled standard unknots give topologically trivial cylindrical cobordisms, with the examples realizing limiting or tight length-width behavior rather than refuting a claim
They are topologically cylindrical cobordisms between standard unknots, with the computed length/width bounds realized sharply in the scaling examples.
The scaled-unknot cobordisms are topological cylinders/concordances chosen to realize the zero/log-2 length and width bounds sharply, not as counterexamples.
The cobordisms are simple Lagrangian cylinders between standard scaled unknots, and the computed lengths/widths sit at sharp boundary values rather than serving as refutations.
They are topologically trivial unknot-cylinder cobordisms used as tight length/width model cases, with one direction arbitrarily short and the reverse governed by the log-2 lower bound.
topologically trivial annular cobordisms between standard unknots whose lengths/widths realize boundary or sharp values, used to illustrate asymmetry rather than refute a claim
Topologically trivial cylindrical concordances between standard scaled unknots, used to show reversal/topological symmetry fails while realizing sharp length/width extremes.
The reversed scaled-unknot cobordisms are topological cylinders used to show reversal symmetry of Lagrangian-cobordism geometry fails, with zero/tight minimal length behavior.
standard unknot-cylinder cobordisms are used as sharp length/width computations rather than to refute a claim, and are topologically trivial cylinders.
Used to show the failure of naive reversal/topological symmetry, with topologically trivial unknot cylinders realizing limiting sharp length-width bounds.
The scaled-unknot cobordisms are topologically trivial cylinders and are used as sharp/minimal length-width test cases rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Front projection in standard contact R^3
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains the front projection using the contact form dz-y dx, semicubical cusps, and Reeb-chord action as crossing height, making the basic Legendrian picture concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works through the standard contact R^3 front projection by using the contact form dz - y dx to identify y with the xz-slope, explaining cusps, smooth lifting, and the relation to the Lagrangian projection/action.

passes.reason: The front projection in standard contact R^3 is the flat homogeneous contact model used for visualization and coordinate explanation.
the front projection in standard contact R^3 is a typical diagrammatic model used to explain cusps, slopes, and Reeb chords
The front projection in standard contact R^3 is used as the ordinary local model/notation for drawing Legendrians.
The front projection in standard contact R^3 is the ordinary generic diagrammatic model used for explanation.
The front projection in standard contact R^3 is the ordinary local model for drawing Legendrians, used only explanatorily.
The front projection in standard contact R^3 is the ordinary generic local model for drawing Legendrians with semicubical cusps.
the front projection in standard contact R^3 is the ordinary local model for depicting Legendrians and is used explanatorily
The front projection in standard contact R^3 is the ordinary local model used to explain slopes and cusps, not a structurally special object.
The front projection in standard contact R^3 is used as an ordinary coordinate/model diagram explaining cusps and Reeb-chord height, not as a failure case.
the front projection in standard contact R^3 is used only as the ordinary local model for explaining the picture.
The standard front projection in contact R^3 is used as the ordinary local model for explaining cusps and Reeb-chord height.
The standard front projection in contact R^3 is an ordinary generic model for drawing Legendrians, used for explanation rather than opposition.

examples.name: Isolated Reeb chord and front-resolution sharpness cases
examples.note: As a sufficient condition for sharpness of the top-end width bound, the speaker mentions Legendrians with an isolated Reeb chord, including those obtained by resolving front diagrams.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the isolated Reeb chord case to justify sharpness by placing a large ball there and then applies this reasoning to front-resolution diagrams via their isolated right-cusp Reeb chords.

passes.reason: The isolated Reeb-chord/front-resolution configurations are sharp width cases because the isolated chord realizes the capacity bound, not adversarial failures.
isolated Reeb chords and resolved-front cases are invoked as sharp cases for the width bound, not as counterexamples
An isolated Reeb chord/front-resolution case is presented precisely as a condition making the width bound sharp.
The isolated Reeb chord/front-resolution cases are cited because they make the width bound sharp.
An isolated Reeb chord/front-resolution case is used because it makes the width bound sharp, i.e. it realizes an extremal equality, not a failure of the theorem.
The isolated Reeb chord/front-resolution cases are singled out because they make the width bound sharp.
an isolated Reeb chord or front-resolution case gives sharpness of the width bound, i.e. a tight case rather than a counterexample
Isolated-Reeb-chord/front-resolution examples form systematic cases where the width bound is sharp, so they are extremal rather than counterexamples.
Legendrians with an isolated Reeb chord or coming from front resolution are presented as sharp equality cases for the width bound.
isolated Reeb-chord and front-resolution cases are cited because they make the width bound sharp, not as counterexamples.
Isolated Reeb chords and front-resolution cases are systematic sharpness cases for the width bound, not sporadic exceptions.
An isolated Reeb chord/front-resolution configuration is cited as a systematic case where the width bound is sharp, not as a counterexample.
